title: Unit 23 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That)

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 39: Relative Pronouns. These are the words that link ideas together. 'The man WHO called', 'The book THAT I read'. In Greek, we have one word that is the absolute lazy-student's dream... and another that is a formal masterpiece. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... introduce us to the king of pronouns.
Γεια σας! That king is 'Pou'. P-O-U. It is the most useful word in the whole language. It is the universal relative pronoun. It never declines, it never changes gender, and it never changes case.
Universal and indeclinable! So if I want to say 'The boy who sings', 'The girl who sings', and 'The children who sing'... I just use 'Pou' for all of them?
Exactly! 'To pai-dée pou tra-ghou-dá-ei'. 'O ándras pou vlé-pō'. 'Ta pai-diá pou ér-khon-tai'. It works for people, things, subjects, and objects. It’s perfect.
'Pou' is definitely the favorite. But... sometimes we see a much longer, more complicated pronoun. 'O o-pói-os'.

Ah, 'O o-pói-os' is our formal relative pronoun. It behaves like an adjective. It MUST match its noun in gender, number, and case. 'O ándras o o-pói-os'... 'Ee ghi-naí-ka ee o-pói-a'... 'To pai-dée to o-pói-o'.
Why would anyone choose 'O o-pói-os' when they could just use 'Pou'?

To be precise! Sometimes, if you have two nouns of different genders, 'Pou' can be ambiguous. But if you use 'O o-pói-os', the gender ending tells the listener exactly which noun you are referring to. It’s also used after prepositions. We say 'Me ton o-pói-on'... with whom.

So 'O o-pói-os' is for precision and prepositions. Pro Tip: Stick to 'Pou' for 95% of your life. Use 'O o-pói-os' when you're writing a formal email or a legal document. Now, Athina... what about 'Whoever' or 'Whatever'?
For 'Whoever', we use 'Ó-poi-os'. It declines just like the regular one. 'Whatever' is 'Ó,tee'. 'Can you give me whatever you have?'... 'Mpo-reís na mou dó-seis ó,tee é-kheis?'
'Ó,tee'. It sounds like a little sigh. This concludes Unit 39. You now have the glue to build complex sentences. Excellent work.
Μπράβο σας! Ο δρόμος είναι δικός σας. Γεια σας!
Excellent work. We’ll see you in Unit 40.
